pattern: Every operation moves exactly k from one position's surplus to another's deficit, so feasibility and cost collapse to whether the per-index differences balance and divide evenly by k.
hints: Look at the difference nums2[i] - nums1[i] for every index; an operation only ever moves k of value from one difference to another.
If k is zero nothing can change, so the arrays must already match; otherwise every difference must be an exact multiple of k, and the positive differences must balance the negative ones.
When it is feasible, the answer is the total of the positive differences divided by k (that many operations supply every deficit exactly).

edge_inputs.description: a mix of increases and decreases that balance out
another balanced case with a different k
minimum length, already equal, any k
k is zero and the arrays already match
k is zero but the arrays differ, so it is impossible
differences balance in total but are not divisible by k
differences are all divisible by k but their total is not zero
values and k near the top of their allowed ranges

stress.description: large arrays with independent random values, mostly infeasible
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    k = rng.randint(0, 100000)
    nums1 = [rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums1, nums2, k]

stress.description: large arrays built from a known-reachable sequence of balanced transfers
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    k = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    nums1 = [rng.randint(1000, 900000000) for _ in range(n)]
    diffs = [0] * n
    for _ in range(min(n, 50)):
        i, j = rng.randrange(n), rng.randrange(n)
        diffs[i] += 1
        diffs[j] -= 1
    nums2 = [nums1[t] + diffs[t] * k for t in range(n)]
    return [nums1, nums2, k]
